extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A thermal incubator system consisting of an insulated styrofoam box equipped with a heatbed, a 0.02 m³/s fan, three DS18S20 high‑precision 1‑Wire temperature sensors, a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B controller, MOSFETs and optocouplers on a custom PCB, and a RabbitMQ communication server. The incubator operates in a laboratory environment, maintaining a target temperature range (LL–UL) with a bang‑bang controller that turns the heatbed on for a heating duration H and waits for a duration C. The system is designed for biological incubation, making it suitable for digital twinning due to its measurable thermal dynamics, controllable heating, networked communication, and open‑source hardware/software stack.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Real‑time monitoring and visualization of temperature and controller states via a time‑series database and dashboard; on‑demand calibration of the thermal model (Model B) using automated calibration routines; anomaly detection and heatbed state estimation through a Kalman filter that infers unmeasured heatbed temperature; what‑if simulation capability using co‑simulation of the physical twin and digital twin to evaluate controller parameter changes; self‑adaptation loop that detects changes in plant behavior, schedules experiments, recalibrates the model, reconfigures the Kalman filter and controller, and optimizes performance. These services target operators, engineers, and researchers who need to monitor, predict, optimize, and control the incubator in real time.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Open‑source Raspberry Pi 4 Model B running Linux, with GPIO pins connected to DS18S20 sensors via a 1‑Wire bus; MOSFET drivers and optocouplers on a custom PCB to isolate the 4 A heatbed current; RabbitMQ message broker (v3.x) deployed on the Pi for inter‑component communication; a low‑level driver written in Python/C that abstracts sensor/actuator I/O and publishes data to RabbitMQ; a bang‑bang controller implemented in Python that consumes temperature messages and publishes fan/heatbed commands; two thermal simulation models (Model A with two free parameters, Model B with four free parameters) implemented in MATLAB/Simulink or Python; a Kalman filter for anomaly detection and heatbed temperature estimation; a time‑series database (e.g., InfluxDB or TimescaleDB) to store telemetry; a Grafana or similar dashboard for real‑time visualization; automated calibration scripts that run Model B fitting; co‑simulation framework (e.g., FMI or custom Python scripts) to run what‑if scenarios; all code and hardware schematics hosted on GitHub (https://github.com/INTO‑CPS‑Association/example‑incubator).

Responsibility scope: the controller handles actuation, the low‑level driver handles sensor/actuator I/O, and the DT components (Kalman filter, calibration, what‑if simulation) consume the published messages to provide monitoring, anomaly detection, and optimization.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The DT is implemented as an event‑driven, message‑based constellation. The Raspberry Pi runs a RabbitMQ server (AMQP) that serves as the communication backbone. The controller publishes control commands and receives sensor data via RabbitMQ; the low‑level driver publishes temperature readings and consumes control commands. The DT components subscribe to these topics: a Kalman filter module performs state estimation, a calibration module runs Model B parameter estimation, and a what‑if simulation module performs co‑simulation using the calibrated model. A time‑series database (not named but implied) stores the telemetry, and a dashboard visualizes real‑time status. Resource management is handled locally on the Pi; the architecture follows a publish/subscribe pattern with a single broker, enabling system‑wide coordination through message queues.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The system integrates with external systems primarily through RabbitMQ, which can be deployed on premises or in the cloud, enabling interoperability via the AMQP protocol. The architecture allows integration with other DTs or enterprise systems by exposing the same AMQP topics, but no concrete integration patterns are described beyond the message broker.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin (DT) constellation comprises a set of computational models and data components that are tightly coupled through a message‑broker based communication layer.  The core models are two analytical thermal models of the incubator: Model A (two free parameters – C_air = 616.56 J/K, G_box = 0.65 J/K) and Model B (four free parameters – C_air = 486.12 J/K, G_box = 0.856 J/K, C_heater = 33.65 J/K, G_heater = 0.87 J/K).  These models are built from differential‑equation based dynamics that capture the heat transfer between the heatbed, the air inside the styrofoam box, and the surrounding environment.  Data are collected from three temperature sensors (T1, T2, T3) that are read periodically by the low‑level driver and published to the RabbitMQ communication server.  The DT uses calibration algorithms that ingest the sensor time‑series, estimate the model parameters, and update the models in real time.  The updated models feed into monitoring algorithms that detect safety violations and trigger supervisory control actions.  The data management approach is event‑driven: sensor readings and control commands are exchanged as JSON messages over RabbitMQ, enabling decoupled, real‑time integration of data and models.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is performed by a fan and a heatbed.  The fan is powered at either 12 V or 18 V, as determined by the controller; the heatbed is driven by a bang‑bang controller that turns the heater on for a duration H (heating duration) and then waits for a duration C (waiting duration) before re‑evaluating the temperature.  The controller receives temperature measurements from the low‑level driver via RabbitMQ, applies the control logic defined by the parameters LL (lower limit), UL (upper limit), H, and C, and publishes control commands back to the driver.  The low‑level driver abstracts the low‑level communication with the PT, implements a safe protocol to prevent unsafe states, and publishes the actuator states to RabbitMQ.  Remote control is achieved through message passing over RabbitMQ, with addressing handled by routing keys that identify the target component (e.g., "controller.command", "driver.state").  Safety constraints are enforced by the safe protocol in the driver and by the controller’s temperature limits LL and UL.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing infrastructure consists of three thermistors: T1 positioned outside the incubator to measure ambient room temperature, T2 placed at the hottest point inside the box, and T3 at the coldest point.  The low‑level driver reads each sensor periodically (sampling interval not explicitly specified in the documents) and publishes the readings to RabbitMQ.  Spatial distribution is such that the average of T2 and T3 is used to estimate the mean internal temperature, while T1 provides a reference for the external environment.  Data transmission is event‑driven: each temperature sample is encapsulated in a JSON message and sent over RabbitMQ to the controller and the DT monitoring layer.  Accuracy and precision specifications are not provided in the documents, but the experimental results indicate temperature discrepancies of up to 7 °C between sensors under nominal fan voltage and 6 °C under higher fan voltage.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is ensured through calibration against experimental data: the free parameters of Model A and Model B are tuned to minimize the error between simulated and measured temperature trajectories. Validation is performed by comparing the calibrated models’ predictions to independent measurement runs, as shown in the calibration plots (Figure 13). Uncertainty in the models arises from parameter estimation errors and sensor noise; these are implicitly addressed by the calibration process. Error detection is handled by the DT’s monitoring component, which checks for unsafe or sub‑optimal system behavior and can trigger fault detectors or supervisory controllers. The low‑level driver’s safe protocol also provides a basic form of error correction by preventing unsafe actuation commands. No explicit Kalman filtering, self‑adaptation loop, or formal quality‑assurance procedures are described in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Temperature data is collected by DS18S20 1‑Wire digital thermometers (accuracy 0.5 °C, range –55 °C to +125 °C). The low‑level driver on the Raspberry Pi reads the sensor values periodically (the exact polling interval is not specified in the documents) and publishes the readings to the RabbitMQ message broker. The messages are consumed by the DT components (controller, visualization dashboard, time‑series database). No explicit data preprocessing or quality‑assurance steps are described; the raw temperature values are forwarded as‑is.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands originate from the DT controller and are sent to the low‑level driver via RabbitMQ. The command payloads include fan on/off, heatbed on/off, heating duration (H), waiting duration (C), lower limit (LL) and upper limit (UL) for temperature. The low‑level driver validates each command using a safe protocol that ensures the PT remains in a safe state before execution. After executing a command, the driver publishes the resulting control state (e.g., fan status, heatbed status, current temperature) back to RabbitMQ so that the controller can observe the PT’s response. The documents do not describe explicit authentication tokens, conflict‑resolution logic, or acknowledgement mechanisms beyond this basic publish/subscribe flow.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DT’s communication backbone is a RabbitMQ 3.x broker running locally on the Raspberry Pi 4 Model B. extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The digital twin lifecycle in the incubator case study follows four main phases: (1) Physical Twin Design and Build – construction of the insulated box, installation of heatbed, fan, Raspberry Pi, DS18S20 sensors, and PCB with MOSFETs and optocouplers; (2) Modelling and Simulation – development of two thermal models (Model A with two free parameters, Model B with four free parameters) to predict internal temperature and heatbed inertia; (3) Calibration and Integration – automatic on‑demand calibration of Model B using data from the low‑level driver and RabbitMQ communication, integration of the Kalman filter for heatbed state estimation, and deployment of the controller (bang‑bang with LL, UL, H, C parameters) on the Raspberry Pi; (4) Runtime Operation and Self‑Adaptation – real‑time visualization via time‑series database, anomaly detection, what‑if co‑simulation, and a self‑adaptation loop that schedules experiments, re‑calibrates the model, re‑configures the Kalman filter and controller, and closes the loop. Transition management is handled by the RabbitMQ message broker, which synchronises data between the physical twin, low‑level driver, controller, and digital twin components, while governance is supported by open‑source version control (GitHub) and documentation of each phase.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process follows a model‑driven, simulation‑centric methodology. First, the physical twin is instrumented and its data streams are captured via the low‑level driver and published to RabbitMQ. The digital twin uses two simulation models; Model B is selected for its superior prediction of heatbed inertia. Calibration is performed automatically on demand by running a calibration routine described in section 3.3, which adjusts the four free parameters of Model B to fit measured temperature data. Quality assurance is achieved through real‑time monitoring and anomaly detection using a Kalman filter that estimates the unmeasured heatbed temperature and provides covariance‑based confidence. Validation is performed by comparing simulation outputs against measured data and by running what‑if co‑simulations that feed historical or real‑time PT data into the DT. Version control is handled via GitHub, where the open‑source hardware schematics, firmware, and simulation code are stored. Evolution management is supported by the self‑adaptation loop: when a new object (e.g., a bucket of cold water) changes plant behaviour, the DT schedules an experiment, collects data, re‑calibrates Model B, re‑configures the Kalman filter, runs what‑if simulations to optimise controller parameters, and finally updates the controller. extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: Security and safety measures include a safe protocol embedded in the low‑level driver that ensures the incubator remains in a safe state by periodically reading temperature data and enforcing safe limits before issuing actuator commands. The controller implements a bang‑bang control strategy with defined lower (LL) and upper (UL) temperature limits, heating duration (H), and waiting duration (C) to prevent overheating. Monitoring components in the DT continuously evaluate system performance against safety thresholds; anomalies detected by the Kalman filter trigger safety actions. The communication between controller, low‑level driver, and RabbitMQ server is synchronized to maintain safe operation, and the DT framework incorporates fail‑safe mechanisms by re‑configuring the controller after calibration or anomaly detection.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**Low-level driver** – Abstracts the low level communication with the sensors and actu
ators.

**Controller** – Implements the control logic required to regulate the temperature inside
the incubator. It receives the measured data and sends commands through the
communication server.


**2.2.1** **The Communication Server**


For the case study, the RabbitMQ [4] server was deployed for synchronisation and communication. With tens of thousands of users, RabbitMQ is one of the most popular
open source message brokers. RabbitMQ is a mature, lightweight, and easy to deploy
on premises and in the cloud.

The controller on the Raspberry Pi is listening from the RabbitMQ server for commands of controlling the fan and the heatbed and at the same time sharing the control
states to the RabbitMQ server.
We choose RabbitMQ because it greatly facilitates the digital twinning process, as
the DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages being passed

**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. **2.1.3** **The Fan** We installed a fan (see fig. 3(c)) inside the box to circulate the air inside the box for making the temperature as uniformly distributed as possible. The fan airflow is about 0 _._ 02m [3] s _[−]_ [1] . With a box volume of 0 _._ 03m [3], the fan should take 2 seconds to move all the air within the box. **2.1.4** **The Raspberry Pi** A Raspberry Pi 4 Model B (see fig. 3(d)) was selected to implement the controller. The General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) connectors on the Raspberry Pi can be used to connect many accessories such as temperature sensors, fan, and the heatbed. Furthermore it supports wireless internet out of the box, with built-in Wi-Fi and Bluetooth, 5 which simplifies deployment of the controller. **2.1.5** **The Temperature Sensor** It is necessary to get feedback from the temperature sensors for feedback control. To do this, a DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometer (see fig. 3(e)) was used for measuring the temperature. This temperature sensor can measure temperatures from -55 °C to +125 °C with an accuracy of 0.5 °C from -10 to +85 °C, which is suitable for the physical incubator. And each DS18S20 has a unique 64-bit serial code, which allows multiple DS18S20s to function on the same 1-Wire bus. Such 1-wire bus support to connect multiple sensors with only one GPIO. In the incubator system, three sensors were deployed for detecting the temperature. **2.1.6** **The PCB Design** Controlling the heatbed requires approximately 4A current which is not supported by the Raspberry Pi. Instead, MOSFETs are used to separate the control signals from the Raspberry Pi and the driving currents. ## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.

The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.

more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.
